title: Trie Insert & Search
code: return node is not null && node.IsEnd;

frames.caption: Start with an empty trie: just the root, representing the empty prefix "". Insert cat, car, dog; then Search("car").
frames.note: A trie keys a tree by characters: each edge is one letter, the path from the root to a node spells a prefix, and an IsEnd flag marks where a stored word ends. The root holds no character. Every operation will cost O(L) in the query length L, independent of how many words are stored — that flat O(L) on prefixes is the entire reason a trie exists.

frames.caption: Insert "cat": no child 'c' on node root → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: Insert "cat": no child 'a' on node 'c' → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: Insert "cat": no child 't' on node 'a' → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: End of "cat" → set IsEnd on the final node. "cat" is now a stored word (the * marker).
frames.note: After the last character we set `IsEnd = true` to mark "a complete word ends exactly here". This boolean is what separates a stored word from a mere prefix: the `ca` node exists but is only a prefix (IsEnd == false), while `cat`, `car`, `dog` are real word-ends. Insert is O(L) in the word length L, independent of how many words the trie already holds.

frames.caption: Insert "car": child 'c' already exists (shared prefix) → just descend into it.
frames.note: No new node here: this edge already exists from a previously inserted word, so we just descend into the existing child. This reuse is the whole point of a trie — `cat` and `car` collapse their common head `c -> a` into one path, so total nodes stay near the number of distinct characters rather than the sum of all word lengths.

frames.caption: Insert "car": child 'a' already exists (shared prefix) → just descend into it.
frames.note: No new node here: this edge already exists from a previously inserted word, so we just descend into the existing child. This reuse is the whole point of a trie — `cat` and `car` collapse their common head `c -> a` into one path, so total nodes stay near the number of distinct characters rather than the sum of all word lengths.

frames.caption: Insert "car": no child 'r' on node 'a' → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: End of "car" → set IsEnd on the final node. "car" is now a stored word (the * marker).
frames.note: After the last character we set `IsEnd = true` to mark "a complete word ends exactly here". This boolean is what separates a stored word from a mere prefix: the `ca` node exists but is only a prefix (IsEnd == false), while `cat`, `car`, `dog` are real word-ends. Insert is O(L) in the word length L, independent of how many words the trie already holds.

frames.caption: Insert "dog": no child 'd' on node root → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: Insert "dog": no child 'o' on node 'd' → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: Insert "dog": no child 'g' on node 'o' → create it and descend.
frames.note: Insert walks one edge per character, CREATING the child node when the edge is missing (`Children[i] ??= new TrieNode()`). The character is encoded by the edge, never stored in the node. Crucially, the first character of "car" reuses the very same `c` and `ca` nodes that "cat" already built — shared prefixes share nodes, which is what bounds a trie's depth to the longest word and makes prefix queries cheap.

frames.caption: End of "dog" → set IsEnd on the final node. "dog" is now a stored word (the * marker).
frames.note: After the last character we set `IsEnd = true` to mark "a complete word ends exactly here". This boolean is what separates a stored word from a mere prefix: the `ca` node exists but is only a prefix (IsEnd == false), while `cat`, `car`, `dog` are real word-ends. Insert is O(L) in the word length L, independent of how many words the trie already holds.

frames.caption: All three words inserted. Notice cat and car share the single c → a path and only branch at the last letter — that prefix sharing is the trie's signature.
frames.note: Worst case a trie has 1 + (total characters across all words) nodes, but shared prefixes collapse common heads into one path. Here cat and car reuse c and ca, so the structure is smaller than the 9 characters inserted would suggest. dog shares nothing, so it gets its own full branch.

frames.caption: Search("car"): follow edge 'c' — the child exists, so descend. (No node is ever created during search.)
frames.note: Search reuses the same edge-by-edge walk as Insert, but it never creates nodes — a missing child short-circuits to false. Here the edge exists, so we descend. Each step is O(1), so the whole walk is O(L) in the query length, with no dependence on the dictionary size N.

frames.caption: Search("car"): follow edge 'a' — the child exists, so descend. (No node is ever created during search.)
frames.note: Search reuses the same edge-by-edge walk as Insert, but it never creates nodes — a missing child short-circuits to false. Here the edge exists, so we descend. Each step is O(1), so the whole walk is O(L) in the query length, with no dependence on the dictionary size N.

frames.caption: Search("car"): follow edge 'r' — the child exists, so descend. (No node is ever created during search.)
frames.note: Search reuses the same edge-by-edge walk as Insert, but it never creates nodes — a missing child short-circuits to false. Here the edge exists, so we descend. Each step is O(1), so the whole walk is O(L) in the query length, with no dependence on the dictionary size N.

frames.caption: Reached the "car" node and it has IsEnd == true → Search returns true. "car" is a real stored word, not just a prefix.
frames.note: The ONLY difference between Search and StartsWith is this final check: Search returns the node's IsEnd (it must be a real word end), while StartsWith would return true on arrival regardless. If we had inserted only "cat", the "ca" node would exist yet IsEnd would be false, so Search("ca") would correctly return false. Forgetting the IsEnd check is the classic trie bug.
